headline: Union Environment Minister reviews Haryana's Air Pollution Action Plan with Chief Minister (Haryana)
fullText:
Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change

Union Environment Minister reviews Haryana's Air Pollution Action Plan with Chief Minister (Haryana)

Shri Bhupender Yadav stresses Strict Enforcement, Accelerated Implementation and Whole-of-Society approach to curb NCR Air Pollution in a time-bound manner

24 JUL 2026 10:21PM by PIB Delhi

Union Minister for Environment, Forest and Climate Change, Shri Bhupender Yadav, today held a comprehensive review meeting with the Chief Minister of Haryana, Shri Nayab Singh Saini, and senior officials of the State Government to assess the progress of ongoing measures aimed at improving air quality in Delhi-NCR. During the meeting, the Minister emphasized the need for accelerated implementation, stronger inter-agency coordination and strict enforcement to ensure substantial reduction in pollution levels before the onset of the winter season. Haryana Minister for Environment and Forest, Shri Rao Narbir Singh, was also present during the meeting.

Emphasizing the urgency of timely action, Shri Bhupender Yadav said, "The window before the winter season must be fully utilized to implement pollution control measures in mission mode. Timely execution, effective coordination and strict enforcement will be critical to achieving visible improvements in Delhi-NCR's air quality."

Reviewing measures to curb Road Dust, the Minister requested that end-to-end paving and greening of roads with local species shrubs/grasses during monsoons be undertaken on priority, with focused attention on pothole identification and repair, construction of footpaths and pavements, roadside greening and deep cleaning of roads.

Shri Yadav requested that all gaps in the deployment of Mechanical Road Sweeping Machines (MRSMs) be bridged by September this year. He further suggested that all MRSMs be geo-tagged to facilitate real-time monitoring of road-cleaning operations and improve accountability.

Reviewing the status of Electric Mobility, the Minister stressed that deployment of electric buses across the NCR municipal corporations in Haryana should be expedited, noting significant gaps in the existing fleet. He congratulated the Haryana Government for the rapid installation of EV charging stations across the seven municipal corporation areas under review.

On the issue of End-of-Life (EoL) vehicles, Shri Yadav requested the authorities to expedite issuance of No Objection Certificates (NOCs) for transfer of eligible EoL vehicles outside the NCR, while simultaneously encouraging scientific scrapping of old and unfit vehicles. He also called for faster replacement of old polluting vehicles with electric vehicles under the Parivartan Scheme.

The Minister requested that Automatic Number Plate Recognition (ANPR) cameras be installed at all fuel stations for effective implementation of the "No PUCC, No Fuel" initiative. Stressing the need to improve urban traffic management, Shri Yadav asked the authorities to identify Traffic Congestion Hotspots on a mission mode and implement suitable mitigation measures at the earliest.

Reviewing Industrial Pollution control measures, Shri Yadav stressed the need for accelerated installation of Online Continuous Emission Monitoring Systems (OCEMS) and Air Pollution Control Devices (APCDs) across industrial units.

The Minister reviewed progress on Construction And Demolition Waste management and requested that establishment of Secondary Collection Centres for C&D waste be expedited. He also stressed that liquidation of Legacy Waste should be undertaken on a war footing. Referring to municipal solid waste (MSW) and Biomass Burning, Shri Yadav called for the constitution of dedicated task forces and emphasized achieving 100 per cent collection of municipal solid waste and biomass to eliminate littering and prevent open burning.

On Stubble Burning, Shri Yadav requested the State Government to bridge gaps in procurement of Crop Residue Management (CRM) machinery and accelerate establishment of Custom Hiring Centres. He emphasized the need for intensive Information, Education and Communication (IEC) campaigns and farmer training programmes to promote effective use of CRM equipment. The Minister suggested that local elected representatives identify stubble-burning hotspots in coordination with Gram Panchayats. He further requested District Collectors to survey industrial units and document their crop residue storage and consumption capacities to facilitate efficient demand-supply management. The State authorities were also advised to ensure adequate availability of crop residue balers.

Underscoring the importance of public participation and coordinated institutional action, Shri Yadav said, "Air pollution can be effectively tackled only through a 'Whole of Government' and 'Whole of Society' approach". Chief Minister (Haryana), Shri Saini, directed State Government officials for timely action on different measures discussed during the meeting. He thanked the Union Minister for his valuable guidance in this matter.

The meeting was attended by the Secretary, Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change; Chairperson, Commission for Air Quality Management (CAQM); senior officers of the Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change and the Government of Haryana. Senior officials from the Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) and the Haryana State Pollution Control Board (HSPCB) were also present.

(Release ID: 2289250)
summaryText:
### OVERVIEW
The Minister of Environment, Forest and Climate Change, Shri Bhupender Yadav, has reviewed Haryana's Air Pollution Action Plan with the Chief Minister of Haryana, Shri Nayab Singh Saini, emphasizing the need for accelerated implementation and strict enforcement to improve air quality in Delhi-NCR before the onset of the winter season.

### KEY HIGHLIGHTS
* The Minister requested end-to-end paving and greening of roads with local species shrubs/grasses during monsoons, as well as deep cleaning of roads, with a deadline for bridging gaps in Mechanical Road Sweeping Machines (MRSMs) by September 2026.
* He suggested that all MRSMs be geo-tagged for real-time monitoring of road-cleaning operations and to improve accountability.
* The Minister requested expedited deployment of electric buses across NCR municipal corporations in Haryana and the rapid installation of EV charging stations.
* He called for the issuance of No Objection Certificates (NOCs) for the transfer of eligible End-of-Life (EoL) vehicles outside the NCR and the promotion of scientific scrapping of old vehicles.
* The Minister asked for the implementation of Automatic Number Plate Recognition (ANPR) cameras at all fuel stations.
* He requested the identification of Traffic Congestion Hotspots and the implementation of suitable mitigation measures.
* The Minister emphasized the need for accelerated installation of Online Continuous Emission Monitoring Systems (OCEMS) and Air Pollution Control Devices (APCDs) across industrial units.
* He requested the establishment of Secondary Collection Centres for Construction And Demolition Waste and the liquidation of Legacy Waste.
* The Minister called for the constitution of dedicated task forces to achieve 100 per cent collection of municipal solid waste and biomass.
* He requested the procurement of Crop Residue Management (CRM) machinery and the establishment of Custom Hiring Centres to tackle stubble burning.
category: Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change

headline: Union Minister for Finance & Corporate Affairs Smt. Nirmala Sitharaman outlines 5Rs of responsive tax governance; Calls for greater tax certainty and reduced litigation at the 167th Income Tax celebrations in New Delhi today
fullText:
Ministry of Finance

Union Minister for Finance & Corporate Affairs Smt. Nirmala Sitharaman outlines 5Rs of responsive tax governance; Calls for greater tax certainty and reduced litigation at the 167th Income Tax celebrations in New Delhi today

The Union Finance Minister reiterates fair, efficient, transparent and taxpayer-centric administration

Income-tax Act, 2025 and new rules and forms mark landmark reforms towards simpler and taxpayer-friendly tax system: Revenue Secretary Shri Arvind Shrivastava

CBDT Chairman Ravi Agarwal highlights progress in faster return processing, grievance redressal and litigation reduction

CBDT advances technology-led tax administration through PAN 2.0, ITBA 2.0, IEC 3.0 and SAKSHAM NUDGE

FM Smt. Sitharaman releases six publications highlighting India's growing leadership in international taxation and taxpayer-centric governance

24 JUL 2026 9:23PM by PIB Delhi

Union Minister for Finance & Corporate Affairs Smt. Nirmala Sitharaman participated in the 167th anniversary of Income Tax Day celebrations by Central Board of Direct Taxes (CBDT) in New Delhi today.

Shri Arvind Srivastava, Secretary, D/o Revenue; Shri Ravi Agrawal, Chairman, CBDT; Shri Vivek Chaturvedi, Chairman, CBIC: Members of CBDT and CBIC and other senior officers and officials of the Department were present on the occasion.

Addressing the 167th Income Tax Day celebrations, Union Finance Minister Smt. Sitharaman emphasised that the role of the Income Tax Department has evolved from tax collection to building a fair, efficient, transparent and taxpayer-centric tax administration. She described the implementation of the Income-tax Act, 2025 as a landmark reform that has simplified the law, reduced compliance costs and strengthened ease of doing business. She also highlighted the enhanced capacity of the e-Filing portal, improved return processing, prompt refunds and significant progress in grievance redressal, while stressing that the Department must move beyond disposal of grievances to analysing recurring issues and preventing them through systemic improvements. She outlined the 5Rs of Responsive Tax Governance - Recognise, Respond, Redress, Reflect and Reform, as guiding principles for taxpayer service, emphasising that honest taxpayers should experience convenience, opportunities to correct bona fide errors and a tax administration that exercises its authority with humility. Stressing that taxpayers are partners in India's development, she called for a continued focus on reducing litigation by strengthening tax certainty.

FM Smt. Sitharaman also underlined the importance of strengthening institutional capacity through modern infrastructure, technology and continuous investment in human capital. She appreciated the progress made in upgrading offices and welfare facilities and called for greater capacity building in emerging areas such as artificial intelligence, data analytics, forensic accounting, international taxation, transfer pricing, digital assets, cybersecurity and leadership development. She urged the Department to deepen voluntary compliance, further simplify the taxpayer experience, strengthen tax certainty, harness technology responsibly, build institutional excellence, reinforce public trust and embrace continuous improvement. Congratulating the officers and staff of the Income Tax Department on the occasion of the 167th Income Tax Day, she expressed confidence that the Department would continue to play a pivotal role in promoting ease of doing business, strengthening taxpayer confidence and supporting India's economic growth.

In his address on the occasion, Shri Arvind Shrivastava, Secretary, D/o Revenue, Ministry of Finance, extended his greetings to the officers and officials of the Income Tax Department and described the implementation of the Income-tax Act, 2025 and the new Income-tax Rules and Forms as landmark reforms towards a simpler, clearer and more taxpayer-friendly tax system, and appreciated the Department's efforts in strengthening taxpayer outreach, grievance redressal, litigation management and technology-driven service delivery. Emphasising the need to leverage technology while keeping taxpayers at the centre of reforms, he called for continued focus on ease of compliance, tax certainty, quality service delivery and innovation, and expressed confidence that the Department would continue to strengthen public trust and contribute to the vision of Viksit Bharat by 2047.

Welcoming the dignitaries on behalf of the Income Tax Department, Shri Ravi Agrawal, recalled the priorities set out by the Union Finance Minister during the previous Income Tax Day celebrations and the expectations articulated at the PRARAMBH programme earlier this year, he outlined the Department's progress over the past year in translating those directions into measurable outcomes. He highlighted the Department's sustained efforts towards reducing litigation, faster disposal of appeals and enhancing tax certainty through Advance Pricing Agreements.

He also noted the achievements in the areas of expeditious return processing, appeal-effect orders and refunds, and strengthening grievance redressal. He further highlighted the Department's achievements in expanding taxpayer outreach through initiatives such as PRARAMBH and Samvaad. He also underscored the Department's continued focus on leveraging technology through initiatives such as PAN 2.0, ITBA 2.0, IEC 3.0, Kar Saathi and the SAKSHAM NUDGE framework to simplify compliance, improve taxpayer experience and strengthen voluntary compliance. He expressed his appreciation to all CBDT verticals, including the Exemptions vertical, and the officers involved in framing the Rules and Forms under the Income-tax Act, 2025. Expressing gratitude for the continued guidance and support of the Hon'ble Finance Minister, he reaffirmed the Department's commitment to building a fair, transparent and trusted tax administration through innovation, collaboration and citizen-centric governance.

The Union Finance Minister also released six flagship publications of the Income Tax Department, reflecting its continued commitment to knowledge sharing, capacity building, transparency and taxpayer-centric governance. These included:

Avlokan, the inaugural Annual Report of the FT&TR Division highlighting India's growing leadership in international taxation;

The Manual on Exchange of Information; the updated Guidance Note on FATCA & CRS;

India's first comprehensive Guidance Note on Crypto Asset Reporting Obligations for Crypto-Asset Service Providers;

The Annual Report of the APA Programme, showcasing the programme's progress; and

Re-engineering Tax Governance, tracing the Department's journey of transformation from paper-based processes to an integrated, technology-driven and taxpayer-centric digital ecosystem.

Four short films showcasing the history of taxation, the Karmyogi Shiksha Path Portal, achievements of Income-Tax department in FY 2025-26 and the reimagining of taxpayer communication in the digital age were also screened during the event.

The function ended with a vote of thanks proposed by Smt. G. Aparna Rao, Member, CBDT. She thanked the Union Finance Minister for gracing the occasion and inspiring the Department with her words of wisdom. She also thanked all the other dignitaries present on the occasion. She also thanked the media and organisers of the event.

Watch the live stream of the 167th Income Tax Day celebration on the official YouTube channel of the Income Tax Department on the link given below:https://t.co/ZJsCKJQImC#IncomeTaxDay@nsitharamanoffc@officeofPCM@FinMinIndia@PIB_India

— Income Tax India (@IncomeTaxIndia) July 24, 2026

📡📡 WATCH LIVE📡📡

Union Minister for Finance & Corporate Affairs Smt. @nsitharaman presides over the 167th Income Tax Day celebrations in New Delhi, today. 👇

📺 https://t.co/oWaZQEvSIn#IncomeTaxDay

— Ministry of Finance (@FinMinIndia) July 24, 2026

Tax certainty is one of the strongest foundations of voluntary compliance. Our long-term objective must shift from ‘litigation management’ to ‘litigation prevention’.

It should rest on 3 pillars:

- Clarity in taxpayer obligations through timely circulars

- Consistency of… pic.twitter.com/bNHkIKJWVa

— Nirmala Sitharaman Office (@nsitharamanoffc) July 24, 2026

I would like the Department to focus on 5 priorities, which could be said as the 5Rs of a Responsive Tax Governance -

1. Recognise every taxpayer's concern promptly, acknowledging that all grievances deserve timely attention.

2. Respond with clarity, courtesy and empathy,… pic.twitter.com/5uOq8i8sIh

Over the coming two decades, our collective aspiration is to realise Viksit Bharat by 2047.

The Income Tax Department will play a vital role in improving ease of doing business, promoting compliance and strengthening trust between taxpayers and the State.

I would encourage the… pic.twitter.com/0cF8lNrkZr

Last year, I had emphasised that a modern tax administration requires not only modern laws and technology, but also modern workplaces, well-equipped offices and a motivated workforce.

I am pleased to see that the Department has made steady progress in advancing these priorities… pic.twitter.com/CJj4CxDsPB

As we continue to invest in world-class infrastructure, we must invest with equal commitment in building the knowledge, skills and leadership capabilities of our officers.

I would encourage strengthened specialised training in AI, data analytics, forensic accounting,… https://t.co/9lfgs7aJuZ

(Release ID: 2289220)

summaryText:
### OVERVIEW
Union Finance Minister Nirmala Sitharaman addressed the 167th Income Tax Day celebrations in New Delhi, reiterating fair, efficient, transparent, and taxpayer‑centric administration and highlighting the landmark Income‑tax Act 2025 reforms and technology‑driven initiatives.

### KEY HIGHLIGHTS
- Event held on **24 July 2026** at 9:23 PM (PIB Delhi).  
- Union Finance Minister **Nirmala Sitharaman** and Revenue Secretary **Arvind Srivastava** attended, along with CBDT Chairman **Ravi Agarwal** and CBIC Chairman **Vivek Chaturvedi**.  
- Emphasis on the **5Rs of Responsive Tax Governance** – Recognise, Respond, Redress, Reflect, Reform.  
- Release of **six flagship publications**:  
  1. *Avlokan* (Annual Report of FT&TR Division)  
  2. Manual on Exchange of Information  
  3. Updated Guidance Note on FATCA & CRS  
  4. First Guidance Note on Crypto‑Asset Reporting Obligations  
  5. Annual Report of the APA Programme  
  6. *Re‑engineering Tax Governance*  
- Launch of technology‑led reforms: **PAN 2.0**, **ITBA 2.0**, **IEC 3.0**, **SAKSHAM NUDGE** framework to simplify compliance and enhance taxpayer experience.  
- Highlight of the **Income‑Tax Act 2025** as a landmark reform aimed at simplifying the law, reducing compliance costs, and strengthening ease of doing business.  
- Commitment to reducing litigation through greater tax certainty and moving from grievance disposal to systemic issue analysis.  
- Appreciation of progress in faster return processing, prompt refunds, and grievance redressal; call for continued investment in AI, data analytics, forensic accounting, international taxation, transfer pricing, digital assets, cybersecurity, and leadership development.  
- Announcement of plans to deepen voluntary compliance, improve taxpayer outreach (e.g., PRARAMBH, Samvaad), and build institutional excellence to support the vision of *Viksit Bharat* by 2047.

headline: SAIL declares financial results for Q1 FY27, posts strong profit
fullText:
Ministry of Steel

SAIL declares financial results for Q1 FY27, posts strong profit

24 JUL 2026 9:23PM by PIB Delhi

Steel Authority of India Limited (SAIL) has declared its financial results today on 24th July, 2026 for the quarter ending 30th June, 2026.

Performance of Q1FY27 at a glance:

Particulars | Unit | Q1 25-26 | Q4 25-26 | Q1 26-27

Crude steel production | Million Tonnes | 4.85 | 5.08 | 4.76

Sales volume | Million Tonnes | 4.55 | 5.32 | 4.16

Revenue from operations | Rs. Crore | 25,921 | 30,813 | 26,246

Earnings before interest, tax, depreciation and amortization (EBITDA) | Rs. Crore | 2,925 | 4,762 | 4,356

Profit before exceptional items and tax | Rs. Crore | 890 | 2,653 | 2,303

Exceptional items | Rs. Crore | 0 | (330) | (144)

Profit before tax (PBT) | Rs. Crore | 890 | 2,324 | 2,159

Profit after tax (PAT) | Rs. Crore | 685 | 1,680 | 1,636

The company reported strong profitability in Q1FY27, with net profit rising by over 138% compared to the corresponding period last year (CPLY). During Q1FY27, SAIL moderated volumes compared to CPLY by advancing certain scheduled repairs and maintenance during the quarter, a step necessitated by geopolitical volatility that disrupted the supply chain across the globe. This forward‑looking & planned intervention is expected to support greater production stability and improved performance in the coming quarters.

Commenting on the results of the first quarter of the current financial year, Chairman & Managing Director, SAIL, Dr. Ashok Kumar Panda said, “Amid global uncertainties, the domestic steel industry demonstrated resilience backed by sustained demand in domestic steel consumption. SAIL, through enhanced operational efficiencies, prudent cost management and focused marketing initiatives, has delivered a significantly profitable first quarter in FY27. The company remains confident of leveraging robust manufacturing capabilities and continues to strengthen performance while capitalizing on the sustained domestic steel demand.”

(Release ID: 2289222)

Read this release in:
summaryText:
### OVERVIEW
Steel Authority of India Limited (SAIL) announced its Q1 FY27 financial results on 24 July 2026, reporting a 138% year‑on‑year increase in net profit to ₹1,636 crore despite lower production and sales volumes due to planned maintenance.

### KEY HIGHLIGHTS
- **Results declaration date:** 24 July 2026 (for quarter ending 30 June 2026).  
- **Net profit (PAT):** ₹1,636 crore, up from ₹685 crore in Q1 FY26 (+138% YoY).  
- **Revenue from operations:** ₹26,246 crore (Q1 FY26: ₹25,921 crore).  
- **EBITDA:** ₹4,356 crore (Q1 FY26: ₹2,925 crore).  
- **Profit before tax (PBT):** ₹2,159 crore (Q1 FY26: ₹890 crore).  
- **Crude steel production:** 4.76 million tonnes (Q1 FY26: 4.85 million tonnes).  
- **Sales volume:** 4.16 million tonnes (Q1 FY26: 4.55 million tonnes).  
- **Volume moderation reason:** Advanced scheduled repairs and maintenance during the quarter, necessitated by geopolitical volatility disrupting global supply chains.  
- **Outlook:** The planned intervention is expected to support greater production stability and improved performance in subsequent quarters.  
- **Chairman’s statement (Dr. Ashok Kumar Panda):** Cited domestic steel demand resilience, enhanced operational efficiencies, prudent cost management, and focused marketing initiatives as drivers of profitability; expressed confidence in leveraging manufacturing capabilities to capitalize on sustained domestic demand.

headline: Ministry of New and Renewable Energy Organises Voluntary Blood Donation Camp
fullText:
Ministry of New and Renewable Energy

Ministry of New and Renewable Energy Organises Voluntary Blood Donation Camp

24 JUL 2026 9:17PM by PIB Delhi

The Ministry of New and Renewable Energy (MNRE) organised a Voluntary Blood Donation Camp at Atal Akshay Urja Bhawan, New Delhi, today, reaffirming its commitment to social responsibility and community welfare.

The camp received an encouraging response from officers and staff of the Ministry. A total of 109 officers and staff members voluntarily participated in the blood donation drive, contributing to the success of the event.

The blood donation camp was inaugurated by the Secretary, Ministry of New and Renewable Energy, Shri Santosh Kumar Sarangi, who lauded the enthusiastic participation of donors. He emphasized that voluntary blood donation is a noble act capable of saving countless lives. Appreciating the spirit of service displayed by the participants, he encouraged everyone to continue supporting such humanitarian initiatives.

A certificate of appreciation was presented to all blood donors in recognition of their selfless contribution.

The Ministry expresses its sincere gratitude to all donors, the organising team, the supporting medical staff for their valuable contribution in making the event a success. The Ministry remains committed to promoting voluntary blood donation and other initiatives that contribute to the health and well-being of society.

"Every Blood Donor is a Hero – Your Donation Can Save Lives."

(Release ID: 2289216)
summaryText:
### OVERVIEW  
The Ministry of New and Renewable Energy (MNRE) organized a voluntary blood donation camp on 24 JUL 2026 at Atal Akshay Urja Bhawan in New Delhi, aiming to promote social responsibility and community welfare through blood donation initiatives.  

### KEY HIGHLIGHTS  
- The event was inaugurated by Shri Santosh Kumar Sarangi, Secretary of MNRE.  
- 109 officers and staff members of MNRE voluntarily participated in the blood donation drive.  
- Certificates of appreciation were presented to all blood donors for their contributions.  
- MNRE expressed gratitude to donors, the organizing team, and medical staff for their roles in the event’s success.  
- The Ministry reiterated its commitment to promoting voluntary blood donation and societal well-being, citing the phrase “Every Blood Donor is a Hero – Your Donation Can Save Lives.”
category: Ministry of New and Renewable Energy

headline: Department of Telecommunications Activates Intra-Circle Roaming (ICR) in Affected Districts of Nagaland, Gujarat, UT of Dadra & Nagar Haveli and Daman & Diu amid Heavy Rainfall and Flooding
fullText:
Ministry of Communications

Department of Telecommunications Activates Intra-Circle Roaming (ICR) in Affected Districts of Nagaland, Gujarat, UT of Dadra & Nagar Haveli and Daman & Diu amid Heavy Rainfall and Flooding

Swift measures taken to ensure uninterrupted telecom connectivity and support emergency operations across disaster-affected region

24 JUL 2026 8:03PM by PIB Delhi

The Department of Telecommunications (DoT) has taken immediate and proactive measures to ensure the continuity of essential communication services across disaster-affected regions in Nagaland, Gujarat and the Union Territory of Dadra & Nagar Haveli and Daman & Diu. Continuous heavy rainfall, landslides, flash floods, and severe inundation have caused extensive damage to critical telecom infrastructure, leading to large-scale network outages and disrupted transmission routes in these areas.

To mitigate communication breakdowns during rescue and relief operations, DoT has invoked emergency response mechanisms under the Standard Operating Procedure (SOP)-2020 for Telecom Services in Response to Disasters and the Unified Licence provisions:

Nagaland (Longleng District): Following incessant rainfall, landslides, and multiple Optical Fiber Cable (OFC) cuts, Longleng becomes the second district in Nagaland after Mon to have ICR activated. Directions have been issued to activate ICR immediately upto 23:59 hrs of 27th July, 2026 or till further instructions, whichever is earlier.

Valsad district of Gujarat, Dadra & Nagar Haveli district and Daman district of UT: Severe flooding has caused major node outages across Dadra & Nagar Haveli, Daman, and adjoining Valsad district. DoT has authorized immediate activation of ICR Immediately upto 23:59 hrs of 27th July, 2026 or till further instructions, whichever is earlier.

The activation of ICR enables mobile subscribers of any provider as well as visiting in-roamers to seamlessly access any available network in locations where their home network is temporarily offline. This ensures optimal utilization of surviving telecom infrastructure and maintains vital connectivity for affected residents, emergency responders, healthcare personnel, and disaster management agencies.

The Department of Telecommunications is continuously monitoring network status through its field units and working with all service providers to expedite full restoration of communication networks in the affected districts.

What is ICR & how to use it:

Intra Circle Roaming (ICR) is a telecom arrangement where a mobile subscriber connects to another operator’s network within the same telecom circle (region). This allows users to access mobile services even if their own operator has no coverage in that specific area. It plays a critical role during natural disasters when mobile infrastructure is damaged.

During the time of disaster, if mobile infrastructure is damaged in a region, DoT issues instructions to Telecom Service Operators to implement ICR.

To ensure ICR works:

Keep "Automatic Network Selection" enabled in your phone settings.

If automatic connection fails;

Go to Settings>Mobile Networks>Network Operators

Manually select another available network

(Release ID: 2289168)

Read this release in:
summaryText:
### OVERVIEW
The Department of Telecommunications (DoT) has activated Intra-Circle Roaming (ICR) in disaster-affected districts of Nagaland, Gujarat, and the Union Territory of Dadra & Nagar Haveli and Daman & Diu to maintain telecom connectivity amid heavy rainfall, landslides, and flooding.

### KEY HIGHLIGHTS
- **Affected Regions**: Longleng district in Nagaland; Valsad district in Gujarat; Dadra & Nagar Haveli, Daman, and Daman districts in the UT of Dadra & Nagar Haveli and Daman & Diu
- **Cause**: Incessant rainfall, landslides, flash floods, and severe inundation causing damage to telecom infrastructure and Optical Fiber Cable (OFC) cuts
- **Activation Period**: ICR activated immediately, valid up to 23:59 hrs on 27th July 2026, or until further instructions
- **Mechanism Used**: Emergency response under SOP-2020 for Telecom Services in Response to Disasters and Unified Licence provisions
- **Purpose**: Ensures uninterrupted communication for residents, emergency responders, healthcare personnel, and disaster management agencies by allowing subscribers to access any available network when their home network is offline
- **User Instructions**: Keep "Automatic Network Selection" enabled; manually select another available network if automatic connection fails

headline: DEVELOPMENT OF INLAND WATERWAYS AND RELATED PROJECTS
fullText:
Ministry of Ports, Shipping and Waterways

DEVELOPMENT OF INLAND WATERWAYS AND RELATED PROJECTS

24 JUL 2026 7:46PM by PIB Delhi

The Government of India has embarked upon a transformational drive to promote Inland Water Transport (IWT) as a key pillar of India's multimodal transport system under Maritime India Vision 2030 and Maritime Amrit Kaal Vision 2047. The primary objective encompasses creating world-class waterway infrastructure through fairway development, multimodal terminals, community jetties, river cruise terminals, regional centres of excellence, digital navigation tools, and green vessel technologies, coupled with seamless integration under the PM Gati Shakti National Master Plan.

The development and regulation of National Waterways (NWs) are spearheaded by the Inland Waterways Authority of India (IWAI), an autonomous organization under the Ministry of Ports, Shipping and Waterways. To improve the Ease of Doing Business and simplify approvals, IWAI has introduced an array of digital reforms:

PANI: Portal for Asset and Navigation Information to facilitate seamless navigation planning.

Jalyan & Navic Portal: A centralized database and digital portal developed on the lines of 'Vahan' and 'Sarathi' for nationwide online registration of vessels and crew, enabling accurate tracking and strategic planning.

CAR-D: Cargo Data Portal for real-time tracking and logistics data integration.

Jal Samriddhi Portal: Dedicated portal supporting commercial and operational activities across National Waterways.

Digital Charts and Hydrographic Surveys: Modern hydrographic charts provided digitally to Mariners for enhanced safety.

These infrastructure, digital, and policy reforms have resulted in the operationalization of 32 National Waterways across the country, laying the foundation for a technology-driven, sustainable, and globally competitive IWT ecosystem that reduces logistics costs, promotes green mobility, and accelerates inclusive economic growth.

LOGISTICS EFFICIENCY & ENVIRONMENTAL COMPARATIVE ANALYSIS

Inland Water Transport serves as an economic and environmentally friendly alternate mode of transport to Rail and Road, significantly reducing congestion on existing transport networks.

Economic Freight Cost Comparison (2014 Price Baseline)

Inland Water Transport (IWT): Rs. 1.06 per Ton-Kilometer (TKm)

Railways: Rs. 1.36 per Ton-Kilometer (TKm)

Highways (Road): Rs. 2.50 per Ton-Kilometer (TKm)

(Source: Ministry of Railways, TTSS, and IWAI)

Environmental Impact & Emission Analysis

Greenhouse Gas (GHG) Emissions: IWT emits 0.0006 Rs/TKm, on par with Railways (0.0006 Rs/TKm) and significantly lower than Roadways (0.0031 Rs/TKm).

Air Pollution Costs: IWT accounts for 0.03 Rs/TKm, compared to 0.0366 Rs/TKm for Railways and 0.202 Rs/TKm for Roadways.

Noise, Soil & Water Pollution: IWT has negligible impact on noise, soil, and water pollution, whereas Road transport incurs 0.0032 Rs/TKm (noise) and 0.005 Rs/TKm (soil/water).

(Source: 12th Five Year Plan, Planning Commission TTS Study, PIANC)

POLICY MEASURES & SECTORAL REFORMS

Jalvahak Scheme: A scheme providing 35% financial incentive to cargo owners and scheduled operators for cargo movement on NW-1, NW-2, and NW-16 via the Indo-Bangladesh Protocol (IBP) route by Inland & Coastal Shipping Limited (ICSL). Approved at a budget of Rs. 95.42 Crore for 3 years.

Tonnage Tax Extension: Announced in the Union Budget on 01.02.2025, extending the special taxation system under the Indian Vessels Act, 2021 to inland vessels operating on NWs, rivers, and canals. Tax is calculated based on vessel size (tonnage) rather than net profit, ensuring stable, predictable, and lower tax burden.

National Waterways Regulations 2025: Notified to provide a transparent regulatory framework that encourages private sector capital investment in building and operating inland waterways terminals and jetties.

Cargo Aggregation & MMLPs: To resolve multimodality challenges arising from lack of industrial concentration along river banks, Multi-Modal Logistics Parks (MMLP) at Varanasi and Integrated Cluster-cum-Logistics Park at Sahibganj are being developed through NHLML (MoRTH). Rail connectivity to 3 MMTs is assigned to M/s Indian Port and Rail Company Ltd.

PSU Cargo Shift Mandate: Over 140 Public Sector Undertakings (PSUs) across key ministries (Petroleum & Natural Gas, Co-operation, Fertilizer, Food & Public Distribution, Heavy Industries, Steel, and Coal) have been engaged to mandate cargo allocation and modal shift to waterways.

The Coastal Shipping Act, 2025: Enacted to modernize regulation at par with international standards, expand vessel chartering entities, reduce compliance burdens, and foster ease of doing business. Effective from 15.03.2026.

Coastal Cargo Promotion Scheme: Announced in Union Budget 2026–27 to increase the share of Inland Waterways and Coastal Shipping from 6% to 12% by 2047.

IBP Route Operationalization: Indo-Bangladesh Protocol route No. 5 & 6 between Maia and Sultanganj has been operationalized with successful trial movements.

REGIONAL WATERWAYS DEVELOPMENT & NATIONWIDE STATUS

Northeast Region (NER) Waterways (NW-2 & NW-16)

In addition to completed works (MMLP Jogighopa terminal, Bogibeel cargo-cum-tourist terminal, Dhubri & Bogibeel customs/immigration buildings, and NH-27 approach road), key ongoing projects include:

Fairway development via Dredging Corporation of India (DCI) on NW-2 (Rs. 143 Crore).

Ship Repair Facility at Pandu (revised cost Rs. 283 Crore).

Tourist Jetty at Bishwanath Ghat & Cargo-cum-Cruise Terminal at Neamati Ghat (Rs. 158 Crore).

Regional Centre of Excellence at Dibrugarh (Rs. 188 Crore).

Steel Gangway with Yoke System at Pandu (Rs. 2.88 Crore).

Regional Office, MSDC, Guest House & ITAT building at Fancy Bazar (Rs. 50.00 Crore).

Link Road connecting Pandu Terminal with Ship Repair Facility (Rs. 55 Crore).

2 Steel Pontoons (Rs. 3.40 Crore) and 2 SDB Vessels (Rs. 2.88 Crore) for NW-2.

Mathematical Modelling of Bogibeel Approach Channel (Rs. 0.60 Crore) and DPR for Community Jetties (Rs. 5.56 Crore).

Southern Region Waterways (Kerala, Andhra Pradesh & Telangana)

Kerala (NW-3, NW-8, NW-9): Continued fairway development on NW-3, NW-8 & NW-9. Reconstruction of navigation lock at Thrikunnapuzha and single-lane bridge at Kovilthottam entrusted to Kerala Govt on deposit basis. 24-hour navigational aids installed (15 in NW-8, 25 in NW-9) along with 4 HDPE jetties in NW-3.

Andhra Pradesh & Telangana (NW-4): Land acquisition completed at Muktyala and Harichandrapuram; under progress at Ibrahimpatnam. Ro-Ro terminals developed at Muktyala and Harischandrapuram. 4 floating pontoon jetties handed over to APTDC; 3 steel floating pontoon jetties under fabrication for Krishna River in Telangana.

Eastern, Western & Northern Region Waterways

Odisha (NW-5, NW-14, NW-64): Regular thalweg surveys, 10 navigational buoys installed in Mahanadi River (NW-64), Talcher-Dhamra-Paradip stretch (NW-5) developed for coal evacuation, and 3 concrete jetties in NW-14 & NW-64.

Karnataka (NW-51, NW-90): Development of Kabini River (NW-51) and Sharavati River (NW-90) for cruise tourism with 3 concrete jetties.

Goa (NW-27, NW-68, NW-111): Mandovi, Zuari, and Cumberjua rivers developed for cruise tourism; 10 concrete jetties (including 4 floating concrete jetties on NW-68) provided.

Gujarat (NW-73, NW-100): Development in Narmada River (NW-73) and Tapi River (NW-100) with 2 floating concrete jetties installed in Narmada and 2 in Tapi.

West Bengal (NW-44, NW-97): Swaroopnagar single lane bridge across Ichamati River (NW-44) completed in March 2026 and inaugurated by Hon'ble PM. Sundarban Waterways (NW-97) 201 km international route maintained under PIWTT.

UT of Jammu & Kashmir: Onshore facilities, floating concrete jetties, and nav aids initiated for Jhelum (NW-49), Chenab (NW-26), and Ravi (NW-84).

Himachal Pradesh & Punjab: Feasibility studies underway for Beas River (NW-17) and Sutlej River (NW-98).

List of Sagarmala- funded Projects in Andhra Pradesh

IWAI initiated fairway development in the Vijayawada–Muktyala stretch (82 km) of NW-4 in 2014. The IWT terminal at Muktyala (NTR District) is completed, while Harischandrapuram (Guntur District) is underway. Updation of DPR for NW-4 (Kakinada–Puducherry Canal integrated with Krishna & Godavari) is ongoing. The Commamuru Canal (113 km from Vijayawada to Pedaganjam Lock) passes through Bapatla District, connecting NW-4 with the North Buckingham Canal towards Chennai.

Under the Sagarmala Scheme, 9 projects worth Rs. 1,286 Crore have been sanctioned for Andhra Pradesh:

Sl. No . | Name of Project | Implementing Agency | Status | District | Project Cost (Rs. in crore) | MoPS W Share (Rs. in crore)

1 | Construction of coastal Berth at Visakhapatnam port | Visakhapatnam Port Authority (VPA) | Completed | Visakhapatna m | 43 | 30

2 | Construction of grade separator from H-7 area to Port connectivity Road by passing Convent Junction - Visakhapatna m Port | National Highways Authority of India (NHAI) | Completed | Visakhapatna m | 60 | 23.17

3 | 2 to 4 laning of port road connectivity to NH – 5 -Phase II | NHAI | Completed | Visakhapatna m | 77.00 | 20.00

4 | Centre of Excellence in Maritime and Shipbuilding - CEMS - AP-18 out of 24 Labs in Andhra Pradesh | Indian Register of Shipping (IRS) | Completed | Visakhapatna m | 574.00 | 36.76

5 | Coastal Districts Skill Development Program - Phase I - Andhra Pradesh | Ministry of Rural Development (DDU-GKY) | Completed | Multiple districts | 0.14 | 0.14

6 | Development of Fishing Harbour in Juvvaladinne in SPSR Nellore District, Andhra Pradesh | Andhra Pradesh Maritime Infrastructure Development Corporation (APMIDCL) | Completed | Nellore | 288 | 69.14

7 | Improvement of Kakinada Anchorage Ports infrastructure in East Godavari district, Andhra Pradesh | Andhra Pradesh Maritime Board | Completed | Kakinada | 85.83 | 36.67

8 | Modernisation of Visakhapatna m Fishing Harbour | VPA | Under Implementatio n | Visakhapatna m | 178.51 | 50.00

9 | Coastal Districts Skill Development Program - Phase 2 - Andhra Pradesh | Ministry of Rural Development (DDU-GKY) | Under Implementatio n | Multiple districts | 5.98 | 5.98

Expected economic benefits include logistics cost reduction, improved evacuation of agriculture/aquaculture produce, supply chain efficiency, and regional employment generation.

HYDROGRAPHIC NAVIGATION & RIS INFRASTRUCTURE

Hydrographic Survey Expansion: Regular weekly, fortnightly, or monthly thalweg surveys are carried out across 22 National Waterways (NW-1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 8, 9, 14, 16, 23, 31, 40, 44, 47, 57, 64, 73, 86, 94, 97, 100, 110) and the Indo-Bangladesh Protocol route to assess navigational channels and fairway conditions.

River Information System (RIS): Implemented on NW-1 (Ganga) across Haldia–Farakka (Phase-I), Farakka–Patna (Phase-II), and Patna–Varanasi (Phase-III). Features 4 Control Stations, 13 Base Stations, AIS, VHF communication, meteorological sensors, and RIS consoles on 30 departmental vessels for real-time traffic monitoring.

Electronic Navigational Charts (ENCs): Prepared and uploaded on PANI and IWAI Vessel Tracker for 17 operational/tidal waterways (NW-1, 3, 5, 8, 9, 10, 14, 23, 27, 53, 64, 68, 73, 83, 86, 91, 110, 111). Prior to 2014, ENCs were available only for NW-1.

ANNEXURE-I: NATIONAL WATERWAYS FINANCIAL & PHYSICAL PROGRESS

Progress of Inland Water Transport Projects/ Schemes on National Waterways:

Sl. | Name of Project | Sanctioned Amount (Rs. in Crore) | Progress as on 30.06.26 (in % )

1 | JMVP-1 on NW-1 (Ganga River) | 5061.15 | 81.97

2 | JMVP-II (Arth Ganga) on NW-1 (Ganga River)

3 | 8 Hybrid Electric Catamaran vessels | 144 | 60.91

4 | Comprehensive Development of NW-2 (Brahmaputra River) | 639.92 | 99.96

5 | Development of Approach Road from Pandu Port Terminal to NH-27 and Ship Repair Facility at Pandu, Guwahati on NW-2 | 419.00 | 96.84 (Approach Road completed)

6 | Comprehensive Development of NW-16 (Barak River) | 134.72 | 48.94

7 | Development of 23 NW's (Phase-1) (3 existing & 20 new NW's) in various States | 266.06 | 90.00

(i) | NW-3-West Coast Canal (Kottapuram - Kollam), Champakara and Udyogmandal Canals in Kerala

(ii) | Part of NW-4- Krishna River (Vijayawada – Muktyala) in Andhra Pradesh

(iii) | NW-5- Dhamra-Paradip via Mangalagadi to Pankopal of Brahmani River in Odisha

(iv) | NW-8 (Alappuzha-Changanassery Canal) in Kerala

(v) | NW-9 (Alappuzha-Athirampuza Canal) in Kerala

(vi) | NW-10 (Amba River) in Maharashtra

(vii) | NW-27 (Cumberjua River) in Goa

(viii) | NW-28 (Dabhol Creek Vashisthi River) in Maharashtra

(ix) | NW-31 (Dhansiri / Chathe River) in Assam

(x) | NW-37 (Gandak River) in U.P. and Bihar

(xi) | NW-40 (Ghaghra River) in U.P. & Bihar

(xii) | NW-44 (Ichamati River) in West Bengal

(xiii) | NW-53 (Kalyan-Thane-Mumbai Waterway, Vasai Creek and Ulhas River System) in Maharashtra

(xiv) | NW-57 (Kopili River -Banthai Gaon Tinali Bus Stop to Chandrapur No.2 confluence with Brahmaputra) in Assam,

(xv) | NW-64 (Mahanadi River) in Odisha

(xvi) | NW-68 (Mandovi River) in Goa

(xvii) | NW-73 (Narmada River) in Gujarat

(xviii) | NW-86- (Rupnarayan River) in West Bengal

(xix) | NW-91 (Shastri River - Jaigad Creek System) in Maharashtra

(xx) | NW-97 (Sundarbans Waterways) in West Bengal

(xxi) | NW-100 (Tapi River) in Gujarat

(xxii) | NW-110 (Yamuna River) in Delhi, Mathura and Vrindavan.

(xxiii) | NW-111 (Zuari River) in Goa

8 | Other 11 National Waterways (NWs) namely; NW-26 (Chenab River), NW-49 (Jhelum River) & NW-84 (Ravi River) in Jammu & Kashmir; NW-46 (Indus River) in Laddhakh, NW-17 (Beas River) & NW-98 (Sutluj River) in Hiamachal Pradesh & Punjab; NW- 51 (Kabini River), NW-52 (Kali River), NW-90 (Sharavati River) & NW-105 (Udayavara River) in Karnataka and NW-14 (Baitrani River) in Odisha | 242.74 | 23.19

Detailed Status of Jal Marg Vikas Project (JMVP) Components on NW-1

Sl. No. | Project | Status

1 | Development of Multi-Modal Terminal at Varanasi, Sahibganj & Haldia | Completed and Operational

2 | Development of new Navigational Lock at Farakka | Completed and Operational

3 | Development of Intermodal Terminal, Kalughat | Completed and Operational

4 | Development of Intermodal Terminal Balagarh | Under Implementation

5 | Modernization of existing Navigational Lock at Farakka | Under Implementation

6 | Installation of 66 Community Jetties in Uttar Pradesh, Bihar, Jharkhand and West Bengal | Completed and Operational

7 | Development of Ship Repair & Maintenance Facility at Patna and Varanasi | Under implementation

8 | Proposed River Cruise Terminals at Patna and Varanasi | DPR/Planning stage

9 | Proposed Water Metro at Varanasi, Patna and Kolkata on NW-1 | DPR/Planning stage

10 | Quick Pontoon Opening Mechanisms (QPOMs) in Uttar Pradesh and Bihar | 2-commissioned and additional 8 under development.

11 | End to end Fairway development along NW-1 in stretches Tribeni-Katwa, Katwa – Farakka, Farakka–Kahalgaon, Sultanganj–Mahenderpur–Barh, Barh–Digha, Digha – Majhaua, Majhaua – Ghazipur, Ghazipur – Varanasi, and Kalughat Access Channel etc. | Ongoing

12 | Development of Regional Centre of Excellence, Varanasi | DPR being prepared

13 | Development of Ro-Pax facility at 4 locations , in the state of Bihar & West Bengal | Commissioned

14 | Procurement of Pusher Tug (3 Nos) + Dumb Barge Flotilla (6 Nos) | Completed

15 | Design construction and supply of 10 Cutter Suction Dredgers (CSDs) | 1 delivered; 9 under development

16 | 4 Hybrid Survey Vessels | Under development

17 | Development of MMLP-Freight Village, Varanasi | Under Tendering stage

ANNEXURE-II: CONSOLIDATED INFRASTRUCTURE & POLICY MEASURES

NW-1 (Ganga River): 66 community jetties, 20 floating terminals, 3 MMTs (Varanasi, Sahibganj, Haldia), 1 IMT (Kalughat), 4 Ro-Pax terminals, 5 pre-existing permanent terminals, new Farakka Navigation Lock, and 2 QPOMs.

NW-2 (Brahmaputra River): 13 floating terminals, MMTs at Pandu & Jogighopa, terminals at Bogibeel & Dhubri, dedicated cruise jetties at Jogighopa, Pandu, Biswanath Ghat, Neamati, and passenger/cruise jetties at Sadiya, Lyka, Orium Ghat.

NW-16 (Barak River) & IBP: Renovated/upgraded terminals at Badarpur & Karimganj; Sonamura terminal on Gumati River (Tripura) on the IBP route.

NW-3 (Kerala): 9 permanent IWT terminals with godowns and 2 Ro-Ro/Ro-Pax terminals.

NW-68 (Goa): 4 floating concrete jetties provided and installed.

Other National Waterways: 4 Tourist Jetties on NW-4 (AP), 8 Floating Jetties on NW-110 (Mathura-Vrindavan), 2 Jetties on NW-73 (Narmada, Gujarat), 2 Jetties on NW-100 (Tapi, Gujarat), and 2 Jetties on NW-37 (Bihar).

Fairway Maintenance: Dredging, river training, channel marking, and hydrographic surveys to maintain 35/45 m channel width and 2.0 / 2.2 / 2.5 / 3.0 m Least Available Depth (LAD).

This information was given by the Union Minister of Ports, Shipping and Waterways, Sarbananda Sonowal, in a written reply to the Lok Sabha.

(Release ID: 2289149)

summaryText:
### OVERVIEW
The Government of India is advancing Inland Water Transport (IWT) as a core component of its multimodal transport system under Maritime India Vision 2030 and Maritime Amrit Kaal Vision 2047, having operationalized 32 National Waterways and introduced digital portals, policy measures, and regional infrastructure projects to create a technology‑driven, sustainable IWT ecosystem.

### KEY HIGHLIGHTS
- Press release issued on 24 JUL 2026 at 7:46 PM by PIB Delhi.  
- 32 National Waterways (NWs) operationalized nationwide.  
- Economic freight cost (2014 price baseline): IWT Rs 1.06/TKm, Railways Rs 1.36/TKm, Highways Rs 2.50/TKm.  
- Greenhouse‑gas emissions: IWT 0.0006 Rs/TKm (on par with Railways) vs Roadways 0.0031 Rs/TKm.  
- Air pollution costs: IWT 0.03 Rs/TKm, Railways 0.0366 Rs/TKm, Roadways 0.202 Rs/TKm.  
- Jalvahak Scheme: 35 % financial incentive for cargo owners/operators on NW‑1, NW‑2, NW‑16 via Indo‑Bangladesh Protocol route; budget Rs 95.42 Crore for 3 years.  
- Tonnage Tax Extension: announced in Union Budget 01.02.2025; extends special taxation under Indian Vessels Act 2021 to inland vessels on NWs, based on tonnage.  
- National Waterways Regulations 2025: notified to provide a transparent regulatory framework for private sector investment.  
- Coastal Shipping Act 2025: enacted, effective 15.03.2026; modernizes regulation, expands chartering entities, reduces compliance burden.  
- Coastal Cargo Promotion Scheme (Union Budget 2026‑27): targets increase in IWT and coastal shipping share from 6 % to 12 % by 2047.  
- Indo‑Bangladesh Protocol Route No. 5 & 6 (Maia–Sultanganj) operationalized with successful trial movements.  
- Regional waterway projects (selected):  
  - NW‑2 fairway development by DCI: Rs 143 Crore.  
  - Ship Repair Facility at Pandu (revised cost): Rs 283 Crore.  
  - Tourist Jetty at Bishwanath Ghat & Cargo‑cum‑Cruise Terminal at Neamati Ghat: Rs 158 Crore.  
  - Regional Centre of Excellence at Dibrugarh: Rs 188 Crore.  
  - Steel Gangway with Yoke System at Pandu: Rs 2.88 Crore.  
  - Regional Office, MSDC, Guest House & ITAT building at Fancy Bazar: Rs 50.00 Crore.  
  - Link Road connecting Pandu Terminal with Ship Repair Facility: Rs 55 Crore.  
  - 2 Steel Pontoons: Rs 3.40 Crore; 2 SDB Vessels: Rs 2.88 Crore.  
  - Mathematical Modelling of Bogibeel Approach Channel: Rs 0.60 Crore; DPR for Community Jetties: Rs 5.56 Crore.  
  - Kerala (NW‑3, NW‑8, NW‑9): continued fairway development; navigation lock reconstruction at Thrikunnapuzha; single‑lane bridge at Kovilthottam; 24‑hour navigational aids installed (15 in NW‑8, 25 in NW‑9); 4 HDPE jetties in NW‑3.  
  - Andhra Pradesh & Telangana (NW‑4): land acquisition completed at Muktyala and Harichandrapuram; Ro‑Ro terminals developed at these sites; 4 floating pontoon jetties handed to APTDC; 3 steel floating pontoon jetties under fabrication for Krishna River in Telangana.  
  - Odisha (NW‑5, NW‑14, NW‑64): regular thalweg surveys; 10 navigational buoys installed in Mahanadi River (NW‑64); Talcher‑Dhamra‑Paradip stretch (NW‑5) developed for coal evacuation; 3 concrete jetties in NW‑14 & NW‑64.  
  - Karnataka (NW‑51, NW‑90): development of Kabini and Sharavati rivers for cruise tourism with 3 concrete jetties each.  
  - Goa (NW‑27, NW‑68, NW‑111): Mandovi, Zuari, Cumberjua rivers developed for cruise tourism; 10 concrete jetties provided (including 4 floating concrete jetties on NW‑68).  
  - Gujarat (NW‑73, NW‑100): development in Narmada River (NW‑73) and Tapi River (NW‑100) with 2 … (as stated in the source).

headline: Two Days of the BRICS Science Academies Forum Meeting 2026 successfully concluded at IIT Hyderabad with focus on AI for Sustainability and Global South Cooperation
fullText:
Ministry of Science & Technology

Two Days of the BRICS Science Academies Forum Meeting 2026 successfully concluded at IIT Hyderabad with focus on AI for Sustainability and Global South Cooperation

24 JUL 2026 7:32PM by PIB Delhi

The Day 01 of the BRICS Science Academies Forum Meeting 2026 with the focal theme “Harnessing Artificial Intelligence for Sustainable Development and Strengthening Global South Cooperation” commenced today at the Indian Institute of Technology (IIT) Hyderabad, bringing together member and participating countries from BRICS to deliberate on the 3C’s Collective, Collaborative and Context sensitive approaches towards Artificial Intelligence (AI). The forum witnessed the presence of 08 BRICS member countries - Brazil, China, Egypt, Ethiopia, Indonesia, Russia and South Africa along with 05 BRICS partner countries - Nigeria, Vietnam, Belarus, Kazakhstan and Malaysia.

Delivering the Presidential Address of the host academy - Indian National Science Academy (INSA), Prof. Shekhar Mande, President of INSA, underscored the transformative role of Artificial Intelligence (AI) in addressing priority global and global south challenges, and also emphasized the importance of ensuring that AI systems remain ethical, inclusive, transparent and accessible to all socio-economic fabric of the society. Dr. Debashis Mitra, Vice President (International Affairs), INSA delivered the context-setting address, underlining the urgency for strengthened multilateral scientific engagement. This was followed by an institutional address by Prof. B. S. Murty, Director of IIT Hyderabad who focussed on the various AI associated programmes undertaken at IIT Hyderabad.

Key Focus Areas of AI Collaboration

The deliberations identified five priority verticals for AI-driven interventions:

Advanced Materials and Manufacturing: Accelerating materials discovery, enabling smart manufacturing and optimizing supply chains.

Healthcare: Enhancing clinical decision-making, enabling population-scale screening and advancing health research.

Climate Change and Environmental Sustainability: Improving long-term forecasting, integrating AI with physical models and enabling risk mitigation.

Agriculture and Food Security: Providing AI-powered advisories, accelerating seed research and leveraging digital public infrastructure.

Disaster Prediction and Management: Strengthening forecasting capabilities, seismic analysis and resource optimization during emergencies.

The thematic focus of the Forum was introduced in the Statement Overview session led by Prof. Ambuj Sagar, IIT Delhi and set the stage for detailed deliberations. Representatives from participating academies presented their national perspectives and engaged in substantive discussions.

Brazilian Academy of Sciences emphasized inclusive AI for healthcare, climate resilience and food security

Chinese Academy of Sciences highlighted data sharing, validation and evidence-based policymaking aligned with SDGs.

Academy of Scientific Research and Technology, Egypt called for capacity building and a BRICS AI Network

Ethiopian Academy of Sciences stressed preservation of linguistic diversity alongside cybersecurity and AI education.

Indonesian Academy of Sciences underlined explainability, ethical risks and the need for AI to augment human judgement.

Russian Academy of Sciences advocated trusted AI systems with strong ethics, transparency and institutional mechanisms such as Centres of Excellence.

Academy of Science of South Africa focused on addressing the digital divide and prioritizing climate, health and food security.

The Nigerian Academy of Science emphasized ethical AI and data-driven planning.

Vietnam Academy of Science and Technology raised concerns on employment, local data and environmental impacts.

National Academy of Sciences of Belarus highlighted telemedicine and data security

National Academy of Sciences of the Republic of Kazakhstan stressed technology access, SDG alignment and support for young scientists.

Summarizing the deliberations, Prof. U. B. Desai, Vice President (Fellowship Affairs), INSA highlighted the need for enhanced cooperation, capacity building and knowledge exchange among member countries. The day also featured a Plenary Lecture by Prof. Anurag Agrawal, Vice President (Science Policy), INSA on the topic “Why AI in healthcare? (And When Not to Use It).” The formal proceedings of Day 01 concluded with discussions, followed by a cultural programme and networking dinner.

The second day of the BRICS Science Academies Forum Meeting 2026 concluded initiated with a plenary lecture by Prof. Indranil Manna, Vice President (Science & Society), INSA. He highlighted the role of AI in materials discovery, including data collection, pattern extraction, physics-based modelling and identification of promising material candidates. The keynote lecture by Dr. Ashish Lele, Director, CSIR-NCL, focused on “Harnessing AI and Green Hydrogen: Driving Efficiency and Decarbonization.” He outlined India’s energy transition pathway, the importance of the National Green Hydrogen Mission and the need for greener logistics and decarbonized data centres.

A key highlight of the concluding day was the Open Discussion on the Joint Statement, chaired by Prof. Shekhar C. Mande, President, INSA, where representatives from BRICS member and partner science academies engaged in extensive deliberations to finalize a consensus communiqué. The Joint Statement, reflecting the shared vision and recommendations of the participating academies, is expected to be endorsed within a week and will be presented at the forthcoming BRICS Science, Technology and Innovation (STI) Ministerial Meeting in August 2026.

In his concluding remarks, Shri Shambhulingappa Hakki, Joint Secretary (MER & G20) and BRICS Sous Sherpa emphasized that science is an indispensable pillar of the Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) and evidence-based public policy. Congratulating the participating academies for arriving at a consensus communiqué, he remarked that the deliberations were both timely and aligned with India's vision of human-centric development.

The proceedings concluded with a vote of thanks by Dr. Brajesh Pandey, Executive Director, INSA who expressed gratitude to all participating academies, delegates, speakers, IIT Hyderabad, MEA and INSA team for concluding the forum successfully.

(Release ID: 2289133)

Read this release in:
summaryText:
### OVERVIEW
The другими iBRICS Science Academies Forum Meeting 2026, held on 23–24 July 2026 at IIT Hyderabad, concluded with a joint communiqué to be endorsed within a week and presented at the upcoming BRICS Science, Technology and Innovation Ministerial Meeting in August 2026. The forum focused on harnessing artificial intelligence for sustainable development and strengthening cooperation among the Global South, with participation from eight BRICS member nations and five partner states.

### KEY HIGHLIGHTS
- **Dates & Venue**: 23–24 July 2026, Indian Institute of Technology Hyderabad; announcement released 24 July 2026 7:32 PM (PIB Delhi).
- **Participants**: 13 academies – BRICS members (Brazil, China, Egypt, Ethiopia, Indonesia, Russia, South Africa, India) and partners (Nigeria, Vietnam, Belarus, Kazakhstan, Malaysia).
- **Theme**: “Harnessing Artificial Intelligence for Sustainable Development and Strengthening Global South Cooperation.”
- **Key Address Speakers**:
  - Prof. Shekhar Mande, President, INSA (Presidential Address).
  - Dr. Debashis Mitra, Vice‑President (International Affairs), INSA (Context‑setting Address).
  - Prof. B. S. wygląda, Director, IIT Hyderabad (Institutional Address).
- **Priority AI Intervention Verticals**:
  1. Advanced Materials & Manufacturing
  2. Healthcare
  3. Climate Change & Environmental Sustainability
  4. Agriculture & Food Security
  5. Disaster Prediction & Management
- **Plenary Lectures**:
  - Prof. Anurag Agrawal (Why AI in healthcare?).
  - Prof. Indranil Manna (AI in materials discovery).
  - Dr. Ashish Lele (Harnessing AI and Green Hydrogen).
- **Joint Statement**: Drafted during the concluding day, to be finalized within one week, and presented at the BR strick STI Ministerial Meeting in August 2026.
- **Official Remarks**: Shri Shambhulingappa Hakki, Joint Secretary (MER & G20) and BRICS Sous Sherpa, highlighted the role ofCarta science in SDG achievement and human‑centric development.
- **Vote of Thanks**: Issued by Dr. Brajesh Pandey, Executive Director, INSA, acknowledging contributions of participating academies, IIT Hyderabad, and INSA staff.

headline: 'Weaves of India' Festival Showcases India's Finest Handloom Heritage at Central Cottage Industries Emporium
fullText:
Ministry of Textiles

'Weaves of India' Festival Showcases India's Finest Handloom Heritage at Central Cottage Industries Emporium

The festival is open to visitors from 24 July to 7 August 2026

24 JUL 2026 7:24PM by PIB Delhi

The Office of the Development Commissioner (Handlooms), Ministry of Textiles, Government of India, is organizing the 'Weaves of India' Festival at the Central Cottage Industries Emporium (CCIE), Janpath, New Delhi. The festival will remain open to the public from 24 July to 7 August 2026 as part of the celebrations leading up to 12thNational Handloom Day on 7th of August.

The festival presents a curated celebration of India's rich and diverse handloom traditions, bringing together exemplary handwoven creations representing 116 heritage weaves from across the country. It offers visitors an opportunity to experience the extraordinary craftsmanship, artistic excellence and cultural legacy preserved by generations of Indian weavers.

The exhibition features celebrated weaving traditions from Odisha, Andhra Pradesh, Uttar Pradesh, Tamil Nadu, Manipur, Chhattisgarh, Bihar, Telangana, Gujarat, Jammu & Kashmir and Tripura, among others. Visitors can explore iconic handloom traditions including Banarasi, Jamdani, Kanchipuram Silk, SambalpuriIkat, Paithani, Kani Pashmina, BawanButi, Kotpad, Ilkal,Kasargod, Himroo and Risha, each representing the unique cultural identity and textile heritage of its region.

Beyond showcasing exceptional textiles, the festival aims to strengthen public appreciation for authentic handwoven products while creating greater awareness about sustainable, ethical and artisan-led production systems. It also provides a valuable platform for interaction between master weavers, designers, collectors, buyers, researchers and the general public.

The Government of India remains committed to preserving, promoting and revitalising the country's rich handloom heritage through sustained support for artisans, design innovation, market development and consumer awareness. The 'Weaves of India' Festival reflects this commitment by celebrating India's living textile traditions and encouraging citizens to support the country's handloom artisans.

The festival is open to visitors at the Central Cottage Industries Emporium, Janpath, New Delhi, from 24 July to 7 August 2026. Members of the public are invited to witness the timeless elegance and unparalleled artistry of India's handwoven heritage.

(Release ID: 2289127)

Read this release in:
summaryText:
### OVERVIEW  
The Ministry of Textiles, Government of India, is organizing the 'Weaves of India' Festival at the Central Cottage Industries Emporium (CCIE), Janpath, New Delhi, from 24 July to 7 August 2026, coinciding with the 12th National Handloom Day on 7 August 2026. The event showcases 116 heritage handloom weaves from 11 Indian states, celebrating the country’s handloom traditions and promoting artisan support and sustainable practices.  

### KEY HIGHLIGHTS  
- **Event Details**: 'Weaves of India' Festival at Central Cottage Industries Emporium (CCIE), Janpath, New Delhi.  
- **Dates**: Open to the public from 24 July to 7 August 2026.  
- **Heritage Weaves Featured**: 116 traditional handloom weaves representing diverse regional crafts.  
- **States Represented**: Odisha, Andhra Pradesh, Uttar Pradesh, Tamil Nadu, Manipur, Chhattisgarh, Bihar, Telangana, Gujarat, Jammu & Kashmir, and Tripura.  
- **Iconic Weaves Highlighted**: Banarasi, Jamdani, Kanchipuram Silk, Sambalpuri Ikat, Paithani, Kani Pashmina, Bawan Buti, Kotpad, Ilkal, Kasargod, Himroo, and Risha.  
- **Purpose**: Strengthen public appreciation for handloom products, raise awareness about sustainable practices, and provide a platform for artisans, designers, and the public to interact.  
- **Government Commitment**: Supports artisans, design innovation, market development, and consumer awareness to preserve India’s handloom heritage.  

ARTICLE:  
Ministry of Textiles'Weaves of India' Festival Showcases India's Finest Handloom Heritage at Central Cottage Industries EmporiumThe festival is open to visitors from 24 July to 7 August 202624 JUL 2026 7:24PM by PIB DelhiThe Office of the Development Commissioner (Handlooms), Ministry of Textiles, Government of India, is organizing the 'Weaves of India' Festival at the Central Cottage Industries Emporium (CCIE), Janpath, New Delhi. The festival will remain open to the public from 24 July to 7 August 2026 as part of the celebrations leading up to 12thNational Handloom Day on 7th of August.The festival presents a curated celebration of India's rich and diverse handloom traditions, bringing together exemplary handwoven creations representing 116 heritage weaves from across the country. It offers visitors an opportunity to experience the extraordinary craftsmanship, artistic excellence and cultural legacy preserved by generations of Indian weavers.The exhibition features celebrated weaving traditions from Odisha, Andhra Pradesh, Uttar Pradesh, Tamil Nadu, Manipur, Chhattisgarh, Bihar, Telangana, Gujarat, Jammu & Kashmir and Tripura, among others. Visitors can explore iconic handloom traditions including Banarasi, Jamdani, Kanchipuram Silk, SambalpuriIkat, Paithani, Kani Pashmina, BawanButi, Kotpad, Ilkal,Kasargod, Himroo and Risha, each representing the unique cultural identity and textile heritage of its region.Beyond showcasing exceptional textiles, the festival aims to strengthen public appreciation for authentic handwoven products while creating greater awareness about sustainable, ethical and artisan-led production systems. It also provides a valuable platform for interaction between master weavers, designers, collectors, buyers, researchers and the general public.The Government of India remains committed to preserving, promoting and revitalising the country's rich handloom heritage through sustained support for artisans, design innovation, market development and consumer awareness. The 'Weaves of India' Festival reflects this commitment by celebrating India's living textile traditions and encouraging citizens to support the country's handloom artisans.The festival is open to visitors at the Central Cottage Industries Emporium, Janpath, New Delhi, from 24 July to 7 August 2026. Members of the public are invited to witness the timeless elegance and unparalleled artistry of India's handwoven heritage.(Release ID: 2289127)Read this release in:

headline: Five Senior Citizen Homes Inaugurated Across Five States; MoU Signed to Strengthen Elderly Welfare; Innovative Programmes Promote Inter-generational Bonding
fullText:
Ministry of Social Justice & Empowerment

Five Senior Citizen Homes Inaugurated Across Five States; MoU Signed to Strengthen Elderly Welfare; Innovative Programmes Promote Inter-generational Bonding

24 JUL 2026 7:19PM by PIB Delhi

The government continues to strengthen its commitment towards ensuring the dignity, security and well-being of senior citizens through comprehensive initiatives under the Atal Vayo Abhyuday Yojana (AVYAY). Commemorating the International Day of Older Persons (IDOP) 2025, the Department organised a series of nationwide programmes to promote healthy ageing, social inclusion, legal awareness and inter-generational bonding among senior citizens.

This information was given by Minister of State for Social Justice and Empowerment Shri B.L. Verma in a written reply to Dr. Ashok Kumar Mittal in the Lok Sabha.

The nationwide celebrations highlighted the Government's sustained efforts to create an age-friendly society while encouraging active participation of senior citizens in community life. The initiatives also complemented the objectives of AVYAY by strengthening partnerships with institutions, civil society organisations and local communities.

As part of the celebrations, a Memorandum of Understanding (MoU) was signed with the Birla Open Minds Foundation to promote collaborative initiatives for the welfare and empowerment of senior citizens. The Department also virtually inaugurated five Senior Citizen Homes located in Tamil Nadu, Maharashtra, Assam, Uttar Pradesh and Jharkhand, further expanding institutional care and support infrastructure for elderly citizens across the country.

To encourage stronger family values and inter-generational engagement, the Department launched "Naitik Patham", an interactive educational game designed to promote meaningful interaction between younger and older generations. A QR Code-based initiative for Grandparents-Teachers Meetings was also introduced to foster greater involvement of senior citizens in children's educational development.

The celebrations included inter-generational bonding programmes in Jabalpur, Badaun, Chhatarpur, Navgaon, Biaora and Mount Abu, bringing together youth and senior citizens through interactive activities. In collaboration with S-VYASA, Bengaluru, yoga sessions and health camps were organised to promote healthy and active ageing among elderly citizens.

Recognising the importance of legal awareness, a special session on the Maintenance and Welfare of Parents and Senior Citizens Act, 2007 was organised in collaboration with the National Legal Services Authority (NALSA). The programme focused on creating awareness regarding the legal rights, welfare provisions and social security measures available to senior citizens while encouraging families and communities to fulfil their responsibilities towards elderly members.

A cultural programme titled "Aradhana" was also organised to celebrate the invaluable contribution of senior citizens to society and to encourage their active participation in cultural and community life.

Through these initiatives, the Government reaffirmed its commitment to ensuring that every senior citizen lives with dignity, respect, security and access to quality care. Under the umbrella of Atal Vayo Abhyuday Yojana (AVYAY), the Ministry continues to work in convergence with State Governments, autonomous bodies and civil society organisations to build an inclusive and supportive ecosystem for senior citizens across the country.

PR – 031 – TAR/KV/GA

(Release ID: 2289125)

summaryText:
### OVERVIEW  
The Ministry of Social Justice & Empowerment inaugurated five Senior Citizen Homes across five states, signed an MoU with the Birla Open Minds Foundation, and launched multiple initiatives under the Atal Vayo Abhyuday Yojana (AVYAY) to commemorate International Day of Older Persons 2025, focusing on elderly welfare, inter-generational bonding, and legal awareness.  

### KEY HIGHLIGHTS  
- Five Senior Citizen Homes inaugurated in Tamil Nadu, Maharashtra, Assam, Uttar Pradesh, and Jharkhand.  
- MoU signed with the Birla Open Minds Foundation to enhance elderly welfare and empowerment.  
- Launch of "Naitik Patham," an interactive educational game to foster inter-generational interaction.  
- Introduction of a QR Code-based "Grandparents-Teachers Meetings" initiative to involve seniors in children's education.  
- Inter-generational bonding programmes held in Jabalpur, Badaun, Chhatarpur, Navgaon, Biaora, and Mount Abu.  
- Collaboration with S-VYASA, Bengaluru, for yoga sessions and health camps to promote active ageing.  
- Legal awareness session on the Maintenance and Welfare of Parents and Senior Citizens Act, 2007, conducted with NALSA.  
- Cultural programme "Aradhana" to celebrate contributions of senior citizens.  
- Initiatives align with AVYAY’s goals to create an age-friendly society through partnerships with institutions and civil society.  
- Press release dated 24 July 2026, issued by PIB Delhi (Release ID: 2289125).
